[b]Vampire Crawlers[/b] managed to capture the same dopamine-fueled loop of constant progression, unlocks, and escalating power of Vampire Survivors while reworking it into something with its own identity through card mechanics and turn-based battles.

The game's progression systems are one of its biggest strengths. Unlocks happen at a constant pace, with new cards, systems, upgrades, characters, and mechanics being introduced frequently enough to keep the experience engaging across its entire runtime.

Combat design is surprisingly clever as well. Infinite combos are kept under control through systems like card cracking and bosses eventually forcing their turn after enough actions, preventing runs from becoming completely automated while still allowing broken builds to exist. The Bridge mode deserves special mention too, since it heavily encourages early-game tempo strategies instead of infinite late-game scaling, helping diversify the gameplay loop and keeping things fresh.

Overall, Vampire Crawlers proves that the success of Vampire Survivors was not simply lightning in a bottle. The developers clearly understand how to build satisfying progression loops and rewarding power fantasies, while still introducing enough new ideas to justify the game's existence. If you're a fan of their previous entry, chances are you'll also enjoy this one.


[h1]The Good[/h1]
+ Excellent meta progression pace with constant unlocks and upgrades after every run
+ Failed runs still feel rewarding thanks to meaningful progression outside of victories
+ Game prevents infinite combos from trivializing the game while still allowing powerful builds
+ Bridge stages are a great addition that encourage different strategies
+ Knowledge from Vampire Survivors is rewarded through things like evolutions
+ Game has just the right amount of content and does not overstay its welcome
+ You can "break the game" after enough investment, just like their previous entry
+ Most achievements unlock naturally through progression and are all very reasonable


[h1]The Neutral[/h1]
* Like Vampire Survivors, the game can become fairly trivial once you fully understand the systems, though experimenting with weaker builds and characters helps keep things fresh


[h1]The Bad[/h1]
- Character balance is not great, with some characters feeling way stronger than others
- Coin Count completely outdamages other options by a huge margin in the endgame

Having completely destroyed Vampire Survivors, I was curious and hopeful as to what poncle would produce next. Surprisingly, they asset reassembled a game that was completely different, identical, familiar, and addictingly engrossing. Perfect price for the amount of 100% achievable game that you receive and yet.. you will pine for more. I hate that I am done already. 

This game as challenging or game-breaking as you want to make it. You can find a happy balance between both with the enabling/disabling of certain upgrades and character builds. There are a lot of unlockables to discover and purchase with the spoils of war. The soundtrack is pretty good, catchy, and keeps things moving. Bugs are near non-existent as far as my personal experience goes, I only encountered one that was a soft lock. I was able to stop and restart the round without quitting and relaunching. The graphics are sprite based, SNES mode-7 type that is good enough to get the job done, this game is about gameplay and convincing yourself to stop after playing 4 hours straight. It doesn't need to look like Cyberpunk 2077 to get the point. There isn't a story. This is about figuring out how to destroy the vampiric hoard using the incantations of cards in a delightful rhythm of ascendingly powerful combos and keeping them going for as long as possible..  and it is ABSOLUTELY A BANGER. 

MAKE AN EXPANSION, MAKE A SEQUEL AND MAKE IT SOON!

"Vampire Survivors, but it's a deck builder"
Primo facie, looks pog af as the kids say (or so I am told). And for a while, it can feel like that.
But then... what... how... am I bored? 
I am. Same old reason: I am not dying. 
As I progress, how to build the deck becomes pretty clear: you make near infinite with draw and energy generation, and when you've played 20 cards you play your big attack and everything dies. What weapon? Doesn't matter (at all). Just play 20 cards before.
The game has a sorry excuse of a mechanic to prevent you from doing a real infinite. It doesn't matter, it's not needed, but you absolutely need to do the near infinite 20 cards thing (which is trivially easy) because no attack will beat the creeps scaling without the (single) combo that is playing cards in order (with bridge cards).
And for a game the demands that, and only that from the player it's UI is surprisingly bad. Because you can't have a very small deck, as the game forces you to take cards (but give you upgrades at the hub to limit how much ends up in your deck) and you need to draw but there is no hand limit, so instead you have very large hands of 20 cards that are painful to navigate and pick. And all you do in the game is trying to click those cards.

After a while it gets really boring.  It's too bad, because with a challenge and game mechanics designed by someone who has beaten ascencion 0 in Slay the Spire, this coud have been quite good. But as it is it feels more like a prototype of a game than an actual one gameplay wise. Which isn't the case of pretty much everything else - art and audio are on point (for this style of game) and the game never crashed. 

Or just as well, maybe this could never have worked. The idea that you could transpose the mess of the orginal games, which is a dumbed down shoot em up, into a deck building game is possiblyhitting a wall here. The original games worked because it was fast, zen, and a frankly silly. This doesn't plug that well in the actual experience of creating a combo deck. For context I relaunched the original game and yup, it's dumb but it's at least a bit challenging and it's fun. This one is less fun. Compelling (it's still a dopamine progression racket, I completed the game) but down the line makes you hate yourself for playing it.

If you like deckbuilding games, Vampire Survivors, and crawling, this is the game for you. Just beat it, still plenty of grind space if I want to max things out but this was satisfying. Looking forward to seeing if they do all content imports from VS. Especially Ode to Castlevania.

Starts out really fun, but as you get a ways into it after several hours the enemies start hard restricting you with some very tedious abilities that try to nerf good deckbuilding. It doesn't really get more difficult, just more annoying, since it drags things down to a crawl for some fights. 

I'm not really here for "challenging" fights anyway, there's a billion other deckbuilders that do that, and better, it would just be nice to have one where you can kinda just power trip through it without having to think too hard about it. That's why I liked Survivors so much and was hoping it'd carry over more.

It's definitely in the same spirit as Vampire Survivors, super easy to get into and ramps up very quickly. And like Survivors they are not asking for much money for this game which is easily worth the price.

Later on though I start to feel it gets really repetitive, this is after you start to unlock multiple crawlers in the same run. you can pretty much assemble the same infinite combos every single encounter until you get to the end of the stage.

Is this different than Survivors, kind of. Obviously all you "do" in Survivors is walk around and watch the screen blow up but I think for this game it's the manual clicking on cards in the right order that makes it feel a lot more repetitive. Still recommend though.

Vampire Crawlers is a fun, pixel art deck builder dungeon crawler that can also technically be called a roguelite, a roguelike, and maybe an action strategy game for good measure. It might sound like a lot, but it works. What makes this game great is that even if you took the Vampire Survivors skin away, you would still have a fantastic game.

Steam Curator: store.steampowered.com/curator/36307721-Healthy-Criticism/

The core gameplay loop is simple: attempt to beat each level, fail, unlock new characters, weapons, bonuses, relics and much more, then try again. Each run can turn out fairly different, even if you use the same crawlers each time.

The crawler roster is comprised of most of the playable characters from Vampire Survivors. They appear as playable cards during battle. The first time a crawler is played they grant a highly rated bonus. After that, they sit on the sideline for a set number of rounds and will not become available until that count is exhausted. The way to bring that count down is by playing their predetermined card colour. Each time that colour is played, not only does the timer decrease, but their respective smaller bonus is also applied, whether that is a projectile, damage, or experience modifier, among many others.

The biggest thing to pay attention to is the bonus build up. Each card costs a set amount of mana, and you start each run with only 2. As you play, you will find ways to increase your total mana pool. Cards need to be played in incremental mana cost order to maximise the damage and modifiers they provide. You must always start with a 0 mana card, then a 1, then a 2, and so forth. The longer you keep the combo going, the more you get out of each card. Planning is essential, because you may have to play a non-damage combo first in order to maximise your overall effectiveness.

You will face enemy waves of 5 per row. The first time an enemy reaches the front row, they take one turn to attack. You must kill, disarm, knock back, or freeze them to avoid taking damage. Bosses await further in and can be quite challenging, so be prepared.

The music is absolutely banging. That is all I have to say about that.

The only issue I have come across is my inability to disable motion blur. I am certain this will be fixed, but across my many hours with the game it is the only legitimate complaint I can find. The only other minor thing is that I really did not care much for the blacksmith and jeweller tents.

To recap, even if you took the Vampire Survivors skin away from this game, you would still have a fantastic deck building, action roguelike, roguelite dungeon crawler. Strategy. At $10 the price is reasonable, but keep in mind that in terms of content, Vampire Survivors has significantly more.

I enjoyed Vampire Survivors. Grudgingly. It spawned an entire genre, sure, and it's a small indie game, but the whole experience felt low rent. The art was clearly "inspired" by real Castlevania sprites and was sometimes mashed together with non-pixel art. I like Roguelikes, but the amount of RNG made the game feel like less of a test of skill than I prefer, and the gameplay boiling down to basic movement was less engaging than I wanted. And there was a lot of pointless flashiness in the design that annoyed me. It's a great game, but a great game in spite of its flaws, at least in my mind.

And all of my complaints are gone for Vampire Crawlers. The art style is more unified. The pixelized 3D really helps with that. (The UI buttons and the title screen still feel amateurish, but it's not a big deal.) The RNG feels right at home in a dungeon crawler RPG. The feeling of "this is almost playing itself" is totally gone: you can very easily screw a run up by picking the wrong upgrades, characters, and arcana cards. And the flashiness compliments the gameplay instead of feeling, to me, like a layer of distracting paint on something very basic.

I downloaded the demo to see if it would slightly irritate me the same way VS did. (I say this having played a lot of VS.) After 3+ hours, I knew the answer was "no."

Really impressive work. I look forward to putting tons more hours into this!

I don't really like card games or deckbuilders outside of Inscryption and Balatro... but this works.

Imagine moving all the weapons, powerups, and upgrade paths, into a dungeon crawler. If you've played Vampire Survivors, you'll feel right at home.

As you level up and defeat bosses, you get to choose from new cards or upgrades to your cards. Different weapons take more mana, and chaining cards with ascending mana costs give you more powerful effects. More damage, more mana, more buffs, etc.

Your gameplay feels pretty rhythmatic. As you familiarize yourself with your deck, your upgrade strategies, and general flow of combos... you start to play faster. Eventually you're just crusing.

The overworld gives you the same permanent upgrades from the first game. Expect to unlock new characters, add gem slots to cards, and unlock new stages/difficulties.

My average playthroughs are around an hour. Maybe less.

Safe to buy this game at full price. Excited to eventually buy more DLC.

It's... okay, I guess? The first two hours are fantastic, but from there on it's a constant repetitions of 0, 1, 2, 3 rinse and repeat.
Leveling up can be undesirable, which is a really weird gameplay decision to me, since it's usually unavoidable. Some characters like not!Bayonetta come with a such a well-preestablished combo to begin with that leveling up actually works against you from the first floor.
Dungeon crawling itself is uninteresting. The floors don't offer enough pathways, enemies all feel the same with slightly bigger or smaller numbers and since most weapons just do damage or do damage an stun, picking one over another doesn't really feel that meaningful when in the end what matter the most is the mana cost.
It also has some oddly outdated decisions like having to scrolls through every building in town to reach the one you want or the exit to menu button being in settings (another building you have to scroll for) instead of just letting the user press scape.

This fixes my main problem with Vampire Survivors, namely that it becomes too brain-dead once you get good at it. This game keeps the same Italian humour and flair, but reworks the Survivor-style hordes into a clever deckbuilder based on numeric combos. It’s tremendously addictive and an absolute blast to play.

My main gripe is that, while Vampire Survivors practically created a genre, this feels like a more derivative product. To its credit, it avoids becoming another shameless Slay the Spire clone in an already oversaturated market, but it still borrows heavily from the StS formula.

Still, it’s a fantastic game overall, and the soundtrack is an absolute banger, as expected.

如果你喜歡那種「一回合直接把整副牌打光」的爽感，那我真的很推薦這款。
整體節奏非常快，戰鬥時間短，核心玩法圍繞在「連擊」上，玩起來會有一種越打越上頭的感覺。

這遊戲的構築思路其實很純粹：
你所有的思考，幾乎都是在研究怎麼把連擊數疊得更誇張。
當牌組開始成形後，真的會進入那種一回合瘋狂連鎖、技能特效停不下來的狀態，看著傷害數字狂跳就是爽。

不過老實說，前期確實有點坐牢。
前期升級都還沒上來時會覺得有些關卡偏硬，甚至偶爾會懷疑人生。 
但這款遊戲你前期坐牢多久，後期就能爽多久。 

到了後期幾乎完全進入「讓玩家爽」的階段：
牌組運轉起來後會有種自己像在開外掛的感覺，尤其成功打出超長連擊時真的超有成就感。
 
是一款非常適合喜歡： 高連擊、爆發流 、快節奏戰鬥、後期成型爽感 
玩家的卡牌遊戲。

Luca Galante ha provato a sperimentare un genere diverso senza voler rinunciare all’anima leggera e assuefacente di Vampire Survivors. Non era facile ripetersi, eppure ci è riuscito alla grande. Il gioco, all’inizio, è veloce, le sessioni sono brevissime e la difficoltà altalenante, esattamente come il suo predecessore, per poi dare il meglio di sé nel late game che richiederà più attenzione e strategia. Ho apprezzato particolarmente questa progressione e mi piace il fatto che per “rompere” il gioco ci voglia un pizzico di pianificazione laddove in VS bastava posare il controller. Lo so, non ha senso fare questi paragoni tra due generi diversi ma i giochi si assomigliano più di quanto possiate pensare nel loro incedere, ed è per questo che considero Vampire Crawlers un vero e proprio sequel che riesce ad essere diverso e più profondo, senza rinunciare a tutto ciò che ha reso popolare il precedente capitolo.

[h1]Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors[/h1]
[h2]✅ Recommandé — [b]MajiNote : A[/b][/h2]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]📌 Contexte[/h2]
[list]
[*][b]Temps de jeu :[/b] ~14h
[*][b]Mode conseillé :[/b] Solo
[*][b]Avis :[/b] Toujours en cours (97/161 succès)
[/list]

[h2]📜 Lecture du Culte[/h2]
Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors est un dungeon crawler / deckbuilder avec des éléments de rogue développé par poncle (les créateurs de Vampire Survivors), en collaboration avec Nosebleed Interactive, sorti en avril 2026.

Le jeu reprend toute la philosophie “dopamine/synergies” de Vampire Survivors… mais dans un délire dungeon crawler + deck building + combos.

[h2]⚙️ La Boucle[/h2]
Tu explores des donjons, tu récupères des cartes, tu construis ton deck, tu débloques du contenu en permanence et tu recommences.

Et le problème du jeu ?

Bah… c’est une drogue.

Le fameux “allez une dernière run” qui finit 4h plus tard.

Le jeu t’attrape et te lâche plus.
La loop est extrêmement intelligente.

[h2]✅ Ce que le Culte approuve[/h2]
[list]
[*][b]L’addiction du gameplay :[/b] c’est catastrophique. Le jeu t’aspire complètement.
[*][b]Les synergies :[/b] c’est là que le jeu devient débile dans le bon sens. Les builds, les interactions, les combos… ça s’enchaîne constamment et c’est ultra satisfaisant.
[*][b]La quantité de contenu :[/b] y a du contenu PARTOUT. Nouveaux chemins, nouvelles cartes, nouveaux persos, nouveaux builds, nouvelles mécaniques… t’as toujours un truc à débloquer.
[*][b]La patte poncle :[/b] on retrouve immédiatement le délire Vampire Survivors. Le jeu comprend parfaitement ce qui rendait déjà leur précédent jeu addictif.
[*][b]L’identité du jeu :[/b] honnêtement c’est un ovni. Dungeon crawler, deckbuilder, rogue… le mélange est bizarre sur le papier et pourtant ça fonctionne extrêmement bien.
[/list]

[h2]⚠️ Ce que le Culte déplore[/h2]
[list]
[*][b]Visuellement… bah ils s’en battent les couilles :[/b] mais en vrai ça fait aussi partie du charme du studio à ce stade.
[*][b]Le jeu peut devenir très “automatique” une fois cassé :[/b] quand les builds commencent à exploser dans tous les sens, le jeu devient parfois plus un simulateur de dopamine qu’un vrai jeu stratégique.
[*][b]Très particulier comme proposition :[/b] je comprends totalement que certaines personnes n’adhèrent pas du tout au délire.
[/list]

[h2]🩸 Moment Culte[/h2]
Le moment où ton build commence à devenir complètement incontrôlable.

Les effets qui proc, les combos qui s’enchaînent, et toi qui comprends que le jeu vient encore de te voler 5h de ta vie.

[h2]🎯 Recommandé si…[/h2]
Tu veux un jeu ultra addictif,
que t’aimes les deckbuilders, les rogues un peu “différents”,
et le fameux délire “allez, juste une dernière run”.

[hr][/hr]

[quote=Maji / Le Culte]
[b]Conclusion :[/b] Je mets A : parce que le jeu est une machine à dopamine absolument terrifiante. Les synergies sont excellentes, le contenu déborde dans tous les sens, et poncle a encore compris comment rendre un jeu ridiculement addictif.

C’est un ovni bizarre, pas toujours très élégant, mais honnêtement… qu’est-ce que c’est bon.

[b]Le Culte valide : A.[/b]
[/quote]

The first word I'd use to describe Vampire Crawlers is "addictive." I don't think that's a good thing. Developer Poncle and Nosebleed really know how to dress up a slot machine. 

Each run starts slow with careful consideration and planning, the goal being to at some point hit escape velocity and have 55 cards in your hand doing 55 million damage, 55 million armor, and 55 million healing. Combining the dopamine neuromancy of Vampire Survivors with it's constant jingling keys and the niche trappings of a dungeon crawler was a stroke of genius. A polypharmaceutical blend of nuanced deckbuilding with a cacophony of pixelated explosions and tsunamis of experience allows Crawlers to set itself apart from the hundreds of other games in the genre that its older sibling built. 

So while it's true that addictive gameplay alone doesn't make for a good game, I'm hopelessly addicted.

Avaliação: Sensacional. Vampire Crawlers mantém a sua identidade do jogo anterior migrando para um formato de cartas que encaixa perfeitamente na proposta roguelike: viciante. 

A regra é simples: cada carta possui uma numeração diferente, e devemos formar combos com essa numeração de forma crescente, ou seja, jogar uma carta 0 > 1 > 2 > 3 e assim por diante. Dessa forma os bônus vão sendo amplificados cada vez mais, o que os tornam essenciais para vencer as hordas cada vez maiores de inimigos e chefes de cada dungeon. Um ponto que talvez incomode um pouco é a duração das dungeons, se resolver matar todo mundo acaba ultrapassando 1 hora de run.

Com o tempo o jogo vai ficando cada vez mais fácil, pois em cada run ganhamos moedas que podemos melhorar diversos pontos diferentes como dano, mana, defesa, mais dinheiro, deck maior, mais sorte, ressuscitar, etc. Além claro de desbloquear e comprar novos personagens, cada um com sua particularidade.

A premissa é bem simples e funciona perfeitamente, com isso as 161 conquistas se tornam muito divertidas de serem adquiridas. Se gostou de Vampire Survivors e nunca deu uma chance pra jogo de cartas essa é a hora, experimente!

作者是天才么，怎么能这么有创意。。同样的素材做出来完全不同的游戏类型，而且内容也很完美。完全不会因为数值问题发愁（点名杀戮尖塔2，到死都抽不到卡我要吐了。）。

游戏难度非常友好，刚购入游戏的时候还会担心新的游戏卡牌玩法会难理解，但是教程和新手关都非常友好，很好上手，没玩过吸血鬼幸存者也能一步一步的理解游戏怎么提升打法。尤其是中间的几个子关卡，也在慢慢的引导新的玩法和打法。

目前的内容也还算丰富了。按照解锁的指引不断的去尝试新的角色，新的卡牌，新的宝石，一点一点的就玩下去了。刚刚开始学会循环之后就会更有趣，20~30小时的游戏时间能慢慢熟悉，直到游戏基本通关。

同时也还是有些缺点，可以以后慢慢更新。比如游戏一局时间有点太长了，要发育牌组，运气好也至少要2层左右才能成型，如果是总计6层左右的关卡估计能玩1小时~1.5个小时才能结束。想象一下一手完全一样的卡牌，对着完全类似的敌人，打一个小时，实在有点枯燥。拿着Steamdeck在床上打，每次都要在中间睡2~3次才能打完。。

还有一些功能或者机制其实不太好懂，有的武器只能打前排，有的能多打一些。他们和一些属性，比如攻击距离+10%这种究竟有没有用其实不能很直观的理解作用。

还有很多游戏说明不太清楚，比如解锁一些成就需要“xx角色达到xx级”这种，如果你跳过了单个角色的阶段，你直接使用2~3个角色同时进入关卡就会发现解锁不了，不管多少次都解锁不了。必须是一个角色进去才能解锁成就。好在其中只有少数3~4个类似的解锁需要尝试究竟要求的是什么，大部分都还好。

另外说到解锁成就，Steamdeack上面的中文文字实在是不能读，可以理解是像素风格的文字，但是在Steamdeck上面很多文字看不清楚，和电脑像素尺寸下的字体差别太大，读起来很累。卡牌看不清，数字看不清，地图也分不清上面是究竟是什么。感觉玩久了眼睛好累。时间久了只能看图片和颜色分别了

After about ten hours, I think I’m ready to share my thoughts. I’m really excited about the game; the first few rounds are stimulating because you unlock a lot of things and the strategies fall into place easily, especially if you have a basic understanding of Survivors. Above all, it’s been a long time since we’ve seen such an original system in “card” games. However, its originality comes at a heavy cost. It quickly becomes boring. There are no challenges; every match feels the same because there are no opponents or in-game situations that force you to rethink your strategy. All the mobs are the same, and the maps hardly change at all (except for the bridges, which were fun). So you get bored—at least I did—and that really gets on my nerves. And having to manually remove your meta progression or set your own challenges doesn’t strike me as good game design. There’s still the hunt for achievements, which is the hallmark of the series. But while in Survivors the matches are constant dopamine hits, here the pace is too choppy, with the dungeon-crawling sections being unengaging. As a result, I don’t see myself grinding because it’s not difficult, just long. In conclusion, I applaud the risk taken and the care put into the game, and I recommend it, but I hope there will be a generous—not greedy—DLC campaign to keep the game fresh with new situations.

[spoiler] 1234512345123451234512345 [/spoiler]

[spoiler] 当发现两张垃圾牌可以合成神器->当发现可以利用宝石效果搓出负数费用的牌达成更高连击->当发现多个英雄一起上场可以接近无限过牌->什么叫还可以给神器打孔上更多宝石->新宝石可以倒序连击！？（我无限了！）->什么叫boss可以鞭尸！？（钱无限了！）->我怎么满手都是万能牌了->我看这死神也不过如此 [/spoiler]

우선 정말 잘 만든 수작이라고 생각합니다. 다만 뱀서에 비해 아쉬운 부분들이 많았습니다.

전작 뱀서에 비해 컨텐츠가 부족하고, 소진 속도가 너무 빠르다고 느껴집니다. 
단순히 스테이지 수가 적을 뿐만 아니라 덱 빌딩의 가짓수가 부족하고, 그중 특정 크롤러 조합이 너무 사기라서 다른 덱 빌딩은 잠금 해제를 위해 억지로 하는 느낌입니다. 

또한 크롤러가 게임 플레이스타일에 끼치는 영향이 너무 적습니다. 결국 0-1-2-3-4-5-와일드 카드 순서의 반복이 되버리며, 이를 막기 위한 보스의 카드 사용 숫자별 기믹도 플레이의 강함에 비해 너무 약해서 죄다 원턴킬이 납니다. 아르카나 또한 효과가 상대적으로 너무 약해서 있으나마나한 아르카나가 대다수이고, 덱 빌딩과 플레이스타일 변화에 아무런 영향을 주지 못합니다.

보라색 사신과 크롤러 효과의 횟수 재한 또한 원턴킬 스타일 변화에 큰 영향을 끼치지 못합니다. 결국 게임이 특정 구간 이후 너무 빠르게 쉬워진다는 인상을 받았습니다. 전작의 경우 한푼한푼 모아가며 파워업을 하고, 조합을 고민해가며 짜는 일련의 힘든 과정들이 길었기 때문에, 시그마를 얻고 치트 수준의 아르카나 조합을 사용했을 때 뽕맛이 더 각별했다고 생각합니다. 이번작은 너무 빠르게 강해져서 강함을 오래 즐기지 못하고 지루해져 버립니다.

그럼에도 엔딩을 볼 때까지는 정말 즐거웠습니다. 또한 전작의 시원한 타격감과 뽕맛은 그대로 와서 너무 좋았습니다. 다만 앞서 서술한 이유들로 인해 지루해지는 타이밍이 빠르게 오기 때문에 전작처럼 긴 시간동안 여러 조합을 시도하며 즐기기는 어렵습니다.  

제 플레이타임 정도의 시간을 때우고 싶으시다면 매우 추천드립니다. 전작을 재미있게 플레이하셨다면 이번작도 분명 재미있을 겁니다.

뱀서를 너무너무 재미있게 했던 입장으로서 적어봅니다.

[h1] Crawling My Way Through [/h1]

[b] What I Liked [/b]
Basically, a new genre has been born with this game.  Never played a game quite like it before.  Many combinations of cards, abilities, and so on.

[b] What I Did Not Like [/b]
There is a constant grind of things to unlock.

[b] Why I Stopped Playing [/b]
Like all games like this, I grew bored after awhile.  It’s a good game, but I had my fill.

[b] Disclaimer [/b]
I have been a gamer for over 30 years.  I tend to play mostly PC games.  I also bounce around between games A LOT.  I may purchase a $70 game and play it for 2 hours or spend $10 on a game and spend 50 hours on it.  Even if I played a game for a short time, it does not mean it is bad.  It just means another game caught my attention.  I also complain about flaws in games a lot, even about the littlest of things.  Keep that in mind when reading my reviews.  If I do not have a complaint, then the game must be special!

Un vrai plaisir et petite nostalgie de Vampire Survivors, le jeu est cool.
Cependant après peu de temps, on comprend vite comment fonctionne le jeu et il n'y a pas 40 façons pour faire d'énormes dégâts donc les runs peuvent vite devenir très répétitives si vous chassez les trophées.

Después de casi 20 años con mi cuenta de Steam, este ha sido mi primer platino. Y creo que eso dice muchísimo de lo que me ha gustado Vampire Crawlers.

Poncle ya demostró con Vampire Survivors que sabían hacer juegos capaces de tragarse cientos de horas, pero ahora han cogido otro de los géneros favoritos del panorama indie —los deck builders— y han vuelto a hacerlo. No solo innovan, también saben divertir.

Lo que más me ha enganchado ha sido la progresión. El gameplay es directo y sencillo, pero cuando empiezas a entender las sinergias, encadenas combos, vuelves a barajar el mazo, lo robas entero y sigues jugando sin perder el combo… la sensación es absurda. Literalmente te sientes como si estuvieras rompiendo el juego y jugando a ser dios mientras la pantalla se llena de números de daño.

Además, el pixel art es una maravilla y el postgame viene cargadísimo de desbloqueos y contenido. Siempre hay “una partida más”. Y otra. Y otra. En Steam Deck es peligrosísimo: consume tan poco que puedes estar 6 horas seguidas con una sola carga sin darte cuenta del tiempo.

Si te gustan los deck builders y los juegos donde la habilidad y las builds rotas se ven recompensadas, este juego es "EL JUEGO".

[b]Vampire Crawlers[/b] is a good game. Everything about it is great, *except* its very core aspect, which is the card part. When you think of a card battler, you think of play possibilities, strategies, builds, etc. But the game fails terribly at that aspect, thus I cannot put a thumbs up. Still, everything else is solid, more on that below.

[h2]The good :[/h2]

- [b]Satisfying gameplay.[/b] The animations and sounds are all very catchy, and keep you in the groove for the entire duration of your runs (which are mostly about combats... lots of them). You can deal insane damage in the endgame, with a satisfying "OVERKILL" announcer ala Quake.

- [b]Great pixel graphics.[/b] Outstanding work by the art team, that really captures the retro feeling from old times.

- [b]Lots of upgrades to help you progress and adjust what cards you find.[/b] Lots of unlockables like hand-size increase, health restoration, etc, to help you clear the harder areas, with an overall good progression curve (but not totally perfect, see the cons).

- [b]Fantastic OST.[/b] The OST is good. Really good. It has that old synthesis vibe from the 90s, and is super catchy, which helps A LOT with the repetitive nature of the game. It's pretty amazing how the music alone can carry a game loop like that. Make sure to buy the DLC Soundtrack if you like it, as it's also on sale on the store page.

[h2]The bad :[/h2]

- [b]Slow start.[/b] The first few hours are slow and may discourage people from continuing. It takes a while (specifically, until you start getting combo cards) until you really pump out the satisfying stuff. But that slow period is carried by the OST (no, I'm serious) so you're still likely to get past it.

- [b]Card battler with zero depth.[/b] The biggest letdown. The game has a lot of cards and heroes (with specific skills) you can use, but the strategy will be the same no matter what : Buff yourself, deal damage, draw cards to replenish your hands, repeat. That's it. And, mind you, that's the *only* strategy possible. There is no way around it. So in the end, it doesn't matter what DPS cards you pick or what hero you take, *anything will work*. You can even clear the last dungeon with the most basic DPS cards if you want.

[h2]Conclusion :[/h2]

A game with everything solid *except* its most important aspect. With no card strategy at all, you're left with a game where you just throw cards without much thinking and watch the big numbers happen. Then you start wondering what's the fun in all this. For the price it's an acceptable time killer but I cannot, in good faith, put a thumbs-up for the former reason. If it's to make such simple gameplay loop, any other genre would've worked, and there was no reason to make it a card game.

[b]Verdict : [/b]If you're here for some mindless demon killing, then that game is for you. If you're here expecting a game like Slay the Spire, Astrea, or Chrono Ark, you'll be sorely disappointed.

🧛‍♂️ Review: Vampire Crawlers – Caos, Cartas e Sobrevivência
Se você é fã da estética clássica de sobrevivência contra hordas e busca algo que adicione uma camada estratégica profunda, Vampire Crawlers entrega exatamente isso. É um vício instantâneo que consegue manter a essência do gênero enquanto inova no sistema de combate.

O que torna o jogo especial:
Atmosfera Impecável: A temática é incrível e respeita muito o estilo visual que amamos no gênero. Ele consegue ser nostálgico e moderno ao mesmo tempo, mantendo você imerso naquela vibe sombria e gótica.

Design de Som Satisfatório: Os efeitos sonoros são um dos grandes destaques. Cada impacto e coleta de recurso gera um feedback auditivo que dá aquela dose de dopamina, fazendo você querer jogar "só mais uma rodada" repetidamente.

Estratégia com Decks: O grande diferencial aqui é a combinação de decks. Não é apenas sobre sobreviver, mas sobre como você monta sua build. A liberdade para criar sinergias entre as cartas permite que cada partida tenha uma identidade própria.

O Caos das Cartas: Ver várias cartas sendo jogadas e ativadas ao mesmo tempo na tela cria um espetáculo visual de destruição. É extremamente recompensador ver o deck que você montou "limpando" a tela de forma frenética.

Veredito:
Vampire Crawlers é obrigatório para quem gosta de teoria de builds e ação ininterrupta. A mistura de deckbuilding com a intensidade de um bullet heaven funciona perfeitamente.

Nota: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Altamente recomendado)

Vampire Crawlers starts out pretty simple but your power escalates quickly as you obtain the meta-progression upgrades, allowing you to break the game in joyous ways just like you did in Vampire Survivors.

When playing cards, they each have a mana cost.  If you keep playing a higher mana cost card in order: 0 cost > 1 cost > 2 cost: this creates a combo chain.  You will find wild cards that can keep the combo going, so you can play 0 cost > 1 cost > 2 cost > wild card > any card can follow.  The higher your combo chain, the more damage and benefits you get out of your cards.

During a run you also find gems to put into your cards to power them up a bit.

The music in the game is great and I was often moving to it while murdering everything on my screen.  It sounds like Castlevania but on crack like it's met with Danny Baranowsky (for the record, Danny isn't one of the composers).  

Vampire Crawlers has become one of my favourite deckbuilders, though I don't see myself playing it again having beaten 93% of the achievements (and killing Red Death).  There are deckbuilders with more replayability potential, but this was great for something short and sweet.

Poncle's Vampire Crawlers remodels the earlier title to accomodate to deckbuilder videogame fans, albeit keeping the original source's very intense paced roguelite inspiration. Don't want to get too much critical here as i still appreciated this game since i played the demo and then immediately resumed progress from there, despite at the moment it is only a rehash of Vampire Survivors' base game but redone on a different key genre. I am already waiting for future updates and DLCs, hoping that this game will take its own direction. If you haven't I would recommend though to check out Vampire Survivors as this is a derivative experience that is best enjoyed if you spent a lot of time enough busting enemies with millions of projectiles filling up the screen.

What really made me enjoy of this game is the heap of player customization options such as the Crawlers (they're the playable characters from the previous game), Arcanas, Gems and [spoiler]the optional feature where you can actually adjust the rarities of gems and adding slots on cards[/spoiler], allowing you to create thousands of different play styles or just simply dig them out of luck. However, unlike Vampire Survivors which you can effortlessly create a build that, if done with enough focus on the attributes of what each item applies, will get you an easy win, later stages will require you attentive concentration on choosing cards, so don't be fooled by the fact this game makes you believe that effortless deckbuilding will let you live.

In other regards, the inclusion of voice acting for the Crawlers has been a surprise for me, sometimes quite repetitive (unless you count on the game's parodic humor which i personally do). I am also happy that Poncle went this far to also have a main theme composed by none other than Yoko Shimomura of Capcom (Street Fighter 2) and Square Enix (Kingdom Hearts, Live A Live) fame.

So far it's not actually a GREAT game in regards, and i only feel like the 9,99€ price tag is a little too high compared to Vampire Survivors' current price tag, but i hope in the future as it updates the actual price will be more worth.

It's fun and I can see potential to become even better as a dungeon crawler/card game. I won't pretend that I know how the game can be better but I feel like the possibilities exist because the dev hit gold twice now.

This is a game I can recommend to anyone, sale or not. Number go up, man.

我从来没玩过幸存者或者地牢类游戏，但是这作仍然显得很有趣，轻松休闲，也不是完全无脑刷。我觉得这作成功之处在于能抓住玩家想要的核心爽点，不给玩家增添负担，给什么都很大方，也没有严重的惩罚机制，上手也比较容易。虽然我到现在还是没明白很多要素是什么意思，但是基本上不影响游玩体验。

中规中矩，难称佳作：
体验结构：游戏的酣畅体验主要是靠数值和视觉的膨胀爆炸效果创造的，但本作既为卡牌游戏，该类游戏天然对策略性和平衡性提出了较高要求。不像前作《吸血鬼幸存者》可以不平衡，爽就完了。开发商没能驾驭这其中的差异和复杂性，简单将前作的体验模型照搬到卡牌这一类型下。导致游戏无论是战斗还是经济，都难有平衡可言，无法支撑深度体验和长时间游玩。
平衡机制：游戏目前的一些平衡机制，如涨水（不输出跳回合会触发，触发4次召唤死神）、重复打出限制（一张牌每回合只能打出2次，第3次打出会碎裂并召唤死神）、跳卡限制（跳过卡牌要消耗资源，若没有就必须选择不需要的牌）等。这些手段治标不治本，且以惩罚的方式来平衡实在难称优雅。
可重玩性：摆上所赐，游戏可重玩价值也较低，打过的图基本不想再打，过图只为解锁新内容。但即便已经通关全图，但仍然剩下一些未获取过的卡牌，而我也没有继续获取的欲望，因为大数字带来的兴奋体验已经彻底疲劳，此时游戏的卡牌战斗对我已经没有任何吸引力。
局外成长：局外强化鸡肋，只是简单的数值提升以优化进图开局体验。所获得的神器只是阶段性的解锁新的功能。英雄有量无质，到后期英雄技能的作用被数值大大稀释，英雄牌逐渐变成污染卡池的废牌。而且由于数值崩溃的连带效应，游戏中期的经济就已经膨胀到没边，金币完全烂大街，一局我就能打10w以上的金币（还懒得鞭尸），这进一步稀释了局外强化的价值和成长体验。
卡牌设计：卡牌看似丰富多彩，还能进化。但实际上有许多定位重复的牌：战术价值是完全相同的，只是视觉效果不一样。不过对于休闲游戏来说，我对其并没有什么卡牌构筑深度上的要求（我认为这款游戏是没有构筑的），这样想的话卡牌效果设计得还不错。
总体来说中规中矩，初见略为上头，但缺乏深度。是一款以解锁新内容和数值爆炸为主要可玩点的休闲游戏。

At first, Vampire Crawlers feels flashy and satisfying. Numbers explode everywhere, builds seem promising, unlocks drip-feed just enough dopamine to keep you clicking “one more run.” But after a while the illusion breaks apart and you realize the game is basically a pachinko machine wearing a Vampire Survivors costume.

Your decisions rarely feel meaningful. Most runs blur together. The “buildcraft” is far more limited than it first appears, and progression becomes less about strategy and more about watching random synergies happen to you. Instead of feeling clever, I mostly felt manipulated into chasing the next tiny unlock.

The biggest problem is the feeling the game leaves behind. Great roguelites make you want to theorycraft, experiment, or immediately start another run because your brain is buzzing with possibilities. Vampire Crawlers left me strangely empty. I would stop playing and feel like I had consumed junk food: momentarily stimulating, but with nothing memorable underneath.

There’s definitely an audience for this kind of turbo-slot-machine design, and some players will enjoy the constant stream of rewards and visual chaos. But if you’re looking for meaningful choices, strong build diversity, or the satisfying “I outsmarted the game” feeling, I don’t think this delivers.

They've done it again, this time translating the simplistic brilliance of VS into a deckbuilder format. poncle and Nosebleed have done an admirable job faithfully translating the items and systems of VS into this new game, making for a very apt extension. Additionally, the core gameplay feel is SUBLIME with some of the most cathartic juice you could hope for.

The main concept of 'Vampire Crawlers' is the stack mechanic. Each card has its own effect ("do X damage", "heal X", etc.), as well as its own cost to play. However, by playing cards in ascending order of cost, you build up a combo multiplier that affects the number on the cards. It's such a basic and simple concept, but it feels amazing, and they add just enough additional mechanics to make sure that you have flexibility in the comboing system while also ensuring there are a number of ways to become overpowered. The same draw of snowballing into a god of death is present, and it's just as great as ever.

Each character has their own starting cards and perks, ensuring variety from run to run. Additionally, you can upgrade cards with gems to further diversify builds. Gems can grant armor/health, increase stats, or even alter the type of card altogether. It's a surprisingly fleshed-out and dense package that has clearly had loads of care put into it. The visual style is also very fun with its 2.5D first-person dungeon crawling.

All that being said, I did feel myself waning on the game after 30 hours, which was surprisingly short, given how many hours I've sunk into VS. Something started to feel a bit repetitive after a while. Additionally, there aren't as many levels/challenges as you'd expect. The main five stages of the original game are present, along with a couple of transitional levels, but that's it.

I'm hoping this is simply the foundation for building on and adding more content, much like they did with VS, because I can see this taking up a lot more of my time as the variety of cards and levels increases. As it stands, it's an easy recommendation for anyone who enjoys VS, rogue-lites, and deckbuilders, particularly.

And just like that, the creators of Vampire Survivors effectively invented a new type of card game. The "Turbo Wildcard" -- a roguelike deckbuilder where the strategy isn't to construct a clean, efficient, small deck -- but put as many cards that are useful in at once. Massive combos. Number go up. Visuals that activate all of your neurons at the same time. Buy it.

On a less "OMG 10/10" note, there are some quality of life features still needed. Card sorting, for example. Both in hand, and while looking at your draw/discard pile. Menu navigation in the town needs work. The gem rarity shop is ill advised to use in general, as it can totally softlock you from winning runs. Vampire Crawlers, also known as The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors by the developer, is a deckbuilding dungeon crawler from Poncle and Nosebleed Interactive. The game builds on the base of Vampire Survivors, but pours it into a totally different genre. Instead of mowing away hordes of enemies in a real-time bullet hell format, step into a dungeon here from a first-person perspective. You move block by block, like in old PC dungeon crawlers. The pace is therefore a lot lower, but the tension does not decrease. The dungeons are full of monsters and objects that we know from Vampire Survivors. Every step you take can start a fight or give a reward. The game knows how to translate the core of its predecessor into the dungeon crawler genre. You recognize the structure and gameplay run right away. At the same time, it feels fresh because of the new approach. That's pretty, because a lot of spin-offs lack that balance. Here it works excellently.

Each floor is about a clear goal. You fight against hordes of enemies, earn experience points in the form of crystals, collect gold and build your deck that you use to defeat the monsters. At the end of the floor, a final boss who tests your build awaits. If you win, you move on to the next floor. This rhythm remains strong throughout the run. It gives a grip and ensures that you always know what you are working towards. The well-known elements from Vampire Survivors are also present. You choose your dungeon based on one of the Vampire Survivors stages each time, select one or more characters with unique weapons, and open chests for upgrades during your run. You use gold for permanent upgrades and achievements unlock new content. Even the music in the dungeons are remixes of the songs you hear in the stages of the same name in Vampire Survivors. All main internships and basic characters from the basic version of Vampire Survivors are present here. That feels familiar and again very addictive. The system keeps you busy for a long time, even when runs fail. The still-a-potty-then-content is therefore very high here.

Vampire Crawlers is a straightforward, simple, and fun little rogue-lite deckbuilder. The different characters introduce a variety of playstyles via their "crawler" card, which, when played, provides a passive bonus and an effect that can be triggered by playing certain other cards. This, combined with a slew of randomly-acquired weapon and powerup cards, gives Vampire Crawlers a nice amount of variety, and it's satisfying to figure out how to make each combination of cards work to your advantage.

That said, the game doesn't provide a significant challenge for those already familiar with deck-building strategy games. It's fairly easy to consistently create incredibly powerful combos and effectively infinite loops after you've learned the game's basics. (There is [i]some[/i] protection against infinite loops, but it's trivial to stop yourself and avoid any penalties before they kick in.) I very rarely failed in my plays, and now that I've "mastered" the game I feel very little need to return.

The UI, while functional, could also use some polish. There's no option to exit the game from town with a quick hit of ESC; instead you have to navigate to the settings menu, which is represented by going to the graveyard in town. I also attempted to re-bind the keyboard inputs from WASD to the more-comfortable-for-me ESDF, but the game wouldn't let me override inputs where the new key I wanted to use was already in-use, so I had to bind everything off of any relevant keys before re-binding things the way I wanted them... only to discover that rebinding the controls didn't also affect menu controls, so no matter what I set my movement buttons to be, menus would still respond to WASD. These are minor grips however, and could easily be fixed with patches in time.

Overall, Vampire Crawlers is a fun, casual deck-builder. It doesn't offer a ton of difficulty or depth, but what's here is nice. I 100%'d the game after about 30 hours of play, and for the low price of the game I'm quite satisfied with that.

[h2]前言 [/h2]
2021年12月，一款并不起眼的复古像素风游戏在steam上悄然开启抢先体验，而在2022年初，当全网玩家都在期待《艾尔登法环》发售时，这款“并不起眼”的Roguelike小游戏却在众多主播发掘传播下，意外火遍了整个游戏圈。

更令人没想到的是，它凭一己之力开辟了全新玩法赛道，自成一派同的时催生出海量同类型作品，让“类幸存者”这个标签变成主流玩法之一，深刻影响了后续无数独立游戏的创作方向。

而如今，在我游玩了《吸血鬼爬行者》后，再一次让我意识到，起初仅有一人的开发团队poncle所打造的《吸血鬼幸存者》，它的成功绝非偶然，制作人他真的懂得从游戏根本出发，用简单不复杂的操作，为玩家带来无比爽快的割草体验。

[h2]依旧内味，依旧爽 [/h2]
如果你玩过《吸血鬼幸存者》，在看到《吸血鬼爬行者》时会发现，游戏的画风依旧是内个复古像素风的味道，但却变成了第一人称卡牌游戏，各种眼熟的武器、道具、敌人，系列素材的重复复用，似乎并没有太多新鲜感。

一开始我也以为，《吸血鬼爬行者》是一款更倾向于Rougelike的DRPG作品，但在我游玩了一小段时间后，它彻底推翻了我的想法。表面上的地牢探索与步步冒险在这游戏里仅仅是套个皮，玩家在一层层地牢里移动，解锁雷达后照着小地图的提示下与打怪、开宝箱、触发事件或是找隐藏物品，本质上游戏没什么探索体验，基本就是照着地图走，有怪打怪，有奖励拿奖励就行了。
而游戏依旧离不开《吸血鬼幸存者》的底层框架——打怪、升级、选武器道具、堆永久性增益等等，让角色的数值逐渐膨胀起来，至于到达一定层数后也会有死神来索命将你带走。

游戏的核心玩法也确实是消耗法力打出不同费用的卡牌来战斗，而且打的是《吸血鬼幸存者》中的武器与道具牌，原本靠升级叠加数值的被动效果，在本作中仅靠打牌就可无限叠加，不同武器卡牌也会受到力量、范围、数量、持续时间所影响，也能虽然看着依然是《吸血鬼幸存者》那套内容，但这些都不是重点。
游戏开始玩家就能获得这个游戏最为核心的神器“煎饼叠叠高”，只要按照卡牌法力消耗，从小到大（0、1.2、3、4）的顺序依次出牌，就能累积连击数，而连击数会成倍增强卡牌数值。

这就意味着，无论是任何卡牌只要上面有带有数值，那么按照法力消耗顺序打出去，每打出一张卡牌的数值都会成倍增涨，在使用镶嵌不同宝石效果的卡牌后，轻松形成无限抽卡无限循环，结合各种的效果增益，往往一张伤害牌能打出成几千乃至上百万的伤害、恢复几十上百的费用。

混杂着游戏令人十分上头的铃铃铃音效，然后你就会彻底放空大脑
只用盯着卡牌上的费用012345012345……不断将手里卡牌打出去，唯一限制玩家的那就是敌人的数量与卡牌破碎。

[h2]局外驱动 [/h2]
游戏的局外内容与《吸血鬼幸存者》一样，在局内战斗达成条件后即可解锁全新物品，通过关卡也能获取神器解锁新建筑，不同建筑都有着独特的功能。

花费金币提升各种属性数值的神殿；解锁购买能力不同、初始卡牌不同的人物，后面在拿到神器后也能三人上阵效果拔群；为卡牌增加孔位的铁匠铺；带来各种效果的卡牌被动。

但这些东西所花费的金币也非常多，当你玩着玩着觉得金币根本不够用时，那么“鞭尸”就出现了，只要手里的牌能循环，那么就能从BOSS尸体上榨取金币，虽说开始只有最多500金币，但解锁“无限鞭尸”后，能打多少就会有多少。

所以《吸血鬼爬行者》提供了丰富了成长驱动力，只要你玩的够久，那么后面卡牌的循环与鞭尸就会让你越爽，这就导致玩完一把意犹未尽又接着玩一把，然后……天就亮了

也因如此，《吸血鬼爬行者》并没有明显的构筑体系，他和前作一样只要你反复游玩解锁东西，在前两层合理运作一番，合成超武、拿到有用的增益牌，基本每一局都能是爽局，但是明显的是由于超武数量有限，反复游玩下来的体验其实又差不多，更多的乐趣是逐渐解锁新东西与成长的过程，以及那纯碎的爽。

同时，游戏目前卡牌读取也有问题，当你反复点击下一回合可能会出卡牌都没出来然后你就跳过手牌的情况，毕竟打来打去节奏太慢，也只有后面解锁倍速会更舒服一点了。

[h2]总结 [/h2]
《吸血鬼爬行者》将《吸血鬼幸存者》的架构融入到这部作品，用卡牌玩法赋予了新的呈现形式，poncle从各方面设计上真的懂得如何让玩家爽，虽然后面反复体验上趋同，但这并不影响它凭借简单的逻辑，赋予玩家极大的正反馈，又一次成为割草解压的佳作。

Vampire Crawlers is basically Vampire Survivors turned into a turn-based dungeon crawler card battler, and it works!

I've put around 30 hours into the game so far, and it's honestly really addictive. The core loop is simple: you go into a dungeon, defeat enemies, gain crystals (XP), level up, and build your deck by choosing between different cards, or sometimes gemstones to improve your build. Depending on what you pick, your whole playstyle will change quickly.

There's also a card evolution system where you can combine certain cards into stronger ones, which is basically the same as Vampire Survivors. It rewards experimenting and figuring things out on your own, since the game doesn't really hold your hands, and i think this is a good thing.

Since it's a roguelike, dying isn't a big deal. You keep your unlocks, gold, and progress, so every run still feels worth it. Over time you unlock new characters (Crawlers), relics, and more options in the village hub, where you can upgrade your runs further, like adding gem sockets to cards or influencing what kind of effects you get more often or not at all.

One of the more interesting mechanics is the combo system. Cards have different mana costs, and playing them in the right order builds up a combo multiplier that makes everything stronger. The deeper you get into the game, the more important this becomes, and it adds a nice layer of strategy.

The visuals are classic pixel art, which fit perfectly, and the sound design is really satisfying. Killing enemies, leveling up, opening chests, it all just feels really good and addictive. There's also a decent variety of enemies and locations, so it doesn't feel too repetitive early on.

What I didn't like is that the card UI can get a bit clunky when your hand gets huge. Sometimes you'll hover over one card and accidentally select another, which can mess you up if you're not careful. Also, runs can feel RNG-heavy at times depending on what you get, but having said that, it's part of the roguelike design and the game itself. Later on it can also get very repetitive, but then again, having payed 9,99 and having over 30 hours in a game, is pretty good in my books.

Other than that, the game feels really clean overall. It's easy to get into, but there's a surprising amount of depth if you stick with it.

If you like roguelikes, deckbuilders, or Vampire Survivors, this is an easy recommendation. It's one of those games where you sit down for one run and suddenly a few hours are gone. Have fun!

Wenn du ein Boomer-Gamer bist, der noch weiß, wie sich echter Grind anfühlt, dann ist Vampire Crawler wahrscheinlich genau dein Ding.
Keine überladene Handholding (außer man legt es darauf an), kein Dauerfeuer an Popups – einfach rein, farmen, stärker werden und Stück für Stück alles auseinandernehmen. Genau diese Oldschool-Motivation hat mich komplett gepackt.

Ja, das Spiel verlangt Zeit. Teilweise auch richtig stumpfen Grind. Aber genau DAS macht den Fortschritt so unglaublich belohnend. Jeder neue Build, jedes seltene Upgrade und jeder Fortschritt fühlt sich verdient an. Man merkt richtig, wie der Charakter langsam zur absoluten Maschine wird.

Für Leute, die wieder dieses „nur noch eine Runde“-Gefühl suchen: absolute Empfehlung.
Perfekte Mischung aus Hirn aus, Monster schnetzeln und trotzdem langfristig motivierendem Progression-System. Man kann nach jeder Ebene das Spiel unterbrechen und später weiter machen.

Genau mein Game.

STEAM DECK
Played using proton 10.0.4 at 800p and default settings. Locked to 60 fps via steam overlay to extend battery life, getting a little over 6 hours easily.

THE GOOD
Addictive gameplay
Funky colors and immersive pixelated graphics
Decent card mechanics
The pace is as fast or slow as you want

THE BAD
Low location variety, mostly just "reused" locations from Vampire Survivors
Last few bosses and unlocks are very grindy
Very little story
Music is cool but gets incredibly repetitive quickly, seems also pretty much reused from Vampire Survivors and I ended up just listening to podcasts instead.

SUMMARY
I can easily recommend Vampire Crawlers for its excellent price.

Just keep in mind that the game is most enjoyable in the meaty middle of the game when you have the most things to unlock but are already able to go to most dungeons. Later it gets quite grindy.

It could definitely use some DLC/adventures to expand on all of its qualities.

First off:
I love this game. Bought it on release for 10 bucks and completed it in a week.
However, there are some things that still need some work:
Some card descriptions aren't quite clear - you sometimes don't know the damage your card will do. 
Secondary effects are not really mentioned in damage calculations. (some cards deal damage after your turn and you don't know how much)
You don't know the hp of any opponents, making the deckbuilding quite difficult.
You also don't know how the stats affect damage.
There is a chance, that you'll be softlocked in some levels - only happened once to me, where the battle didn't register that all enemies are dead and ending the turn just resulted in my next turn.
And the coin-based damage is absolutely busted. I one-shot the main boss with the coin-based damage, which was quite the suprise.

With all of that said, these are the only flaws I found. And to summarize, these are just quality of life changes. The mechanics are really well executed and I was especially surprised with the sound design. There also only was one achievement which, in my opinion, is too tedious, which is "play 3000 purple cards". In one playthrough, I used 1200 purple cards. 
I love the artstyle. I love the mechanics. I love the upgrades.
All in all, a great game! 9/10

【元評価】
デッキ構築のローグライクの基準を人気のあるStSにした場合、難易度という面でヴァンクロは完全に初心者向けといえる。
当然StSで勝てるような思考・工夫ができる人や、デッキ次第でひりつく戦闘となるバランスを求める人には物足りないゲームだろう、低評価の多くはそういった難易度面の印象だ。

ただ考えて欲しい、これはあのヴァンサバをベースとしたゲームであるという事を。
お手軽操作と派手な演出に爽快感、まさに格安インディーゲームの金字塔にまで登り詰めたあのゲームのコンセプトは、目を三角にして針の穴を刺すようなデッキ構築をするゲームではなかった。
そしてヴァンクロも見事にあの雰囲気や難易度を踏襲して、誰でも手に取りやすいゲームになっている。

ヴァンサバ経験者はあの武器、あのキャラ、あの曲、あのエフェクト、色々と思いを馳せるだろう。
未経験でも全く問題ない。なんならヴァンクロからスタートしてヴァンサバに興味を持つのも良いだろう。

序盤はオート(全リリース)プレイで問題なくサクサク進む。
中盤からはカードのコストとコンボを意識すれば概ね問題ない。
アンロック要素とデッキ構築次第ではほとんどの敵に無双状態になれるが、これもまたヴァンサバのバランスを思い出させるもので、見事に再現しているといえる。
発動回数を伸ばして射出した武器のエフェクトで画面真っ白なんていうのもザラにある。

通常のクリアまで大体15～25時間程度のプレイが目安と思われる。
ただ実績などを含めたアンロック要素をやり込むともう少しかかるだろうが、価格とプレイ時間のバランスの目安としてほしい。

ヴァンサバでもあった呪い要素もあるので好みの設定にできるが、まだまだこの辺は物足りない。
またMAPのブロック移動の要素は現状では機能しているとまでは言えず若干のストレス要素。
それこそStS形式の選択ルートに割り切るか、ヴァンサバシステムを利用したフリー移動でも良かったかもしれない。
村での操作性も良くない、各機能へ飛べるショートカット要素は後々のアプデで追加される気がするが。

そんなこんなで比較的とっつきやすいローグライクデッキ構築ゲーム、不慣れな方にこそオススメしたい。

Once you unlock the ability to create a team of 3 Crawlers, the sheer options for combos you can set up and execute gets even crazier.  Also, it's a flashy, brand new game that will RUN ON A POTATO. Replay value and breakability are maybe the only weak parts; there currently aren't many upgrades or evolutions, so once you've beaten all 12 maps or so, there's nothing to do.  Also, once you unlock Pugnala and the Coin Count Gem, you can set up infinite loops and one-shot almost every endgame boss, very very easily, so the last half of the game had almost no real challenge to it.

Games have always been the art form that lets us flirt with oblivion on our own terms—building something fragile, watching it spiral into glorious chaos, and laughing at the wreckage. Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors understands that better than most. It takes the snowballing madness of the original and twists it into a turn-based roguelite deckbuilder where you’re not some invincible hero; you’re a gothic swarm architect, piecing together vampire cards in first-person dungeon crawls that feel like fever dreams soaked in pixel blood.

Every run you’re chasing those busted synergies that turn a polite little blood-sucking collective into screen-filling apocalyptic nonsense. One Turboturn combo later and the dungeon is dissolving while you cackle like a madman. It’s philosophy in motion: creation through controlled chaos, power through inevitable dilution, immortality bought one more ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ run at a time. The hunger mechanics hit especially hard—feed too little and your swarm withers into nihilistic sludge; feed too much and they bloat, betray each other, and remind you that every craving eventually devours the host. Darkly hilarious when it all implodes.

Sure, it carries that familiar Survivors weight of eternity—some runs blur into the same delicious grind, unlocking cards and upgrades while your real-life to-do list quietly bleeds out. But that’s part of the irreverent charm. The game knows it’s a gloriously broken casino of gothic vermin. You bet everything on a hand of cards, then watch a single silver trap or bad draw end your god-run in spectacular fashion. The art nails that PS1 nightmare fuel mixed with cartoon gore—adorable little crawlers twitching in ways that are equal parts cute and deeply cursed. And those “♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥” moments when everything clicks? Pure interactive art: you set the conditions for beautiful disaster, then step back and let the swarm do its thing.

I’ve poured over 15 hours into this beautiful mess and I’m still finding new ways to break it. The progression loop is relentless in the best way—new levels, cards, upgrades, secrets—keeping the blood flowing even when repetition starts nipping at your heels. It doesn’t pretend to be a profound strategy masterpiece. It just crawls up to the abyss, flips it off, and drinks deep while dropping the occasional existential one-liner that lands harder than it should.

If you get why games matter—why we keep coming back to rehearse hunger, power, failure, and ridiculous victory—crawl into this one. It’s addictive in the way only the best vices are. Not flawless, but better than flawless. It’s honest.

I think I enjoyed this and got my money's worth out of it, but it's very much reminding me of Vampire Survivors to the degree where the game gets extraordinarily easy & boring after a certain point.

The moment you unlock any type of consistent Wild card (or the Wildcard gem) the game is over and you can no longer actually lose. It then turns into battling with the UI to click a bunch of single digit numbers in order until you win. Later in runs your main opponent will be deck and hand bloat, sometimes reaching points where it's impossible (and not all that important, really) to see what cards you're even playing. 

The first few hours tease more of an involved game than you wind up getting. It's weird, I've never really felt deceived by a game before like this.

Overall like I said, 20 hours for $10 is fine, but I haven't "finished" the game and I'm bored. I can't hit "don't recommend" for something I've enjoyed enough to spend almost a day playing. Still, don't expect Slay the Spire even if people keep invoking it.

2時間ほど遊んだが、つまらなすぎでこれ以上遊ぶ気がしなくなったのでこの時点でレビューを書く。
正直言って、デッキ構築型ローグライクとしてはゴミ。色々考えてプレイする楽しみの部分をほぼ全て削り、爽快感に全振りされている。
まず、ゲームバランスが終わってる。攻撃が大正義で防御系は使っても誤差でしかないので無いほうがマシ。
カード報酬のスキップが無く、初期デッキにカードを2,3枚追加した時点で半ループが可能なので、戦闘は基本的にデメリットしかない。リスクをとって報酬を得るという基本が機能していない。
ストーリーを進めることで、これから色々な機能が開放されるような感じがあるが、開放速度が遅すぎる上、これ以上強くなってどうするんだ感が強く魅力を感じない。
また、UIも悪く、手札が増えるとごちゃついてカードを使用しづらい。マップの移動もしづらい。ミニマップもあるが、視点移動しないとわからない情報が多く、あまり機能していない。

ここまで悪い部分ばかり書いたが、良い部分もある。
演出の派手さである。
目が痛くなるので、私にとってはそれも悪い部分であるが一般的に歯良い部分だろう。

結論、デッキ構築型ローグライクを求める私のような人には全く刺さらないが、他の評価も見ると高評も多いので、前作ヴァンサバのパチンコ感を求める人には面白く遊べるのだと思う。

Took me only 16 hours to beat the "official final boss" (not deaths) and see the ending. It's very fun as long as it lasts, BUT there's not enough depth in the game to keep my interest past those 16 hours, maybe I'll return to it for a few hours more just to "break" the game and kill the deaths, but it's a bit too grindy and luck based for me to bother for now. 

The balance of the crawlers and arcana is all over the place, some options feel definitely stronger than by other, sometimes by far. The crawlers that give you the passives that deal damage are useless - 40 damage for using a card when you hit for thousands is just pathetic. The Arcana that increases your orbs for every 100 cards used is strongest by far, you easily get 10+ orbs by the last floor, there's little reason to use any other arcana other than for going for some very specific builds (most of which would only work with a combination of two arcanas, so you'd need to be lucky to hit the right second one in the run itself). 

To be fair, I'm not one of those who plays games like Slay the Spire for hundreds of hours, either. I lose my interest after killing the final boss on a normal difficulty, maybe with some additional challenges, as by this point you mostly figured out the game. And Vampire Survivors didn't keep me for that long, either. 

I should also mention, that not being familiar with Vampire Survivors would be a demerit and would increase the learning curve, but I think it's not that much of a problem for most of you, ha-ha. 

Overall, it's a nice game for the price, and I'm sure there's going to be more interesting content and zones to complete, making it even more worth it in the long-term.

每个人都告诉我这玩的很爽。但说实话，越到后面越难受。
随着经验获取和等级上升越来越容易，选取卡牌奖励也越来越频繁的打断我的连击。一个主打连击的游戏，居然随着游戏的进行会越来越不顺畅..就不能在战斗结束后选取卡牌，或在战斗进行中自行选择进行选牌吗？
不仅选牌影响连击体验，越到后面不断臃肿的手牌也是个问题，既没法消耗，也没有排序。每次都要来回查找自己的手牌，打出合适的那一张。没有消耗还算可以接受，但这么大的手牌量甚至都没有排序，简直折磨。
而且连击连到后面，卡牌的破碎进度不是很明显，并且破碎时的卡顿也很不流畅，你要着急想出下一张牌的话会点不出去。
目前的爽感基本来自大数字和鞭尸了，主推的连击体验有点糟糕，顿感十足，而且目前急需手牌排序

The game start fun, it's fast and addictive, but after you start unlocking upgrades, it become way too easy and it happend super fast. There is basically no challenge, you can create infinite loop every run and it's not even hard to figure it out. You can 100% the game in around 15 hours and yet it still felt repetitive. 
It is not a good deckbuilder, and it is not a satisfying survivor since it's just overkilling last enemy while managing a hand way too crowded and avoiding to break you cards by playing them too much.

Eu já curtia muito a fórmula clássica de Vampire Survivors, mas confesso que o estilo proposto por esse novo jogo da poncle nunca havia me chamado a atenção anteriormente. Mas resolvi experimentar a demo por gostar muito de VS, e acabei adorando esse jogo.
Esse jogo ainda tem aquela sensação de progressão viciante do VS e adiciona uma camada de estratégia com as cartas que funciona perfeitamente. No começo pode parecer um pouco lento, mas logo você se vê construindo decks poderosos, fazendo combos altos e limpando ondas gigantescas de inimigos.

Mesmo se você acha que não gosta de jogos de cartas, dê uma chance a este. É viciante do início ao fim. Recomendo demais!

extremamente satisfatório e viciante! pra quem gostou do survivors, esse jogo é um prato cheio, uma bela homenagem ao outro jogo dessa equipe de desenvolvedores. Utiliza as mesmas armas, mesmos equipamentos, mesma lógica de powerups, mas readequados pra mecânica de carteado.

Pra quem gosta de deck builders, esse jogo tem tudo pra fisgar logo de cara. A quantidade de combos e associações possíveis é incrível, cada gameplay é única, porque além de deck builder é um rogue lite (na mesma ideia do vampire survivors), então você adquire habilidades novas a cada run, adquire melhorias permanentes, libera novos personagens jogáveis, com novas armas, tudo isso genialmente implementado com a mecânica de compra de cartas e montagem de deck.

Jogo 10/10, satisfatório, desafiador na medida certa em alguns momentos. Basicamente é a mesma roupagem do Vampire Survivors, mas com uma mecânica de gameplay totalmente diferente, viciante e que demanda muito mais raciocínio e atuação do jogador. 

(Obs final: não dá pra deixar de falar do preço. Jogo extremamente barato. Apesar de possuir gráficos simples, tem uma mecanica diferenciada, inovadora e que funciona muito bem. Considerando o que tem saído pro mercado nessa faixa de preço, provavelmente é disparado a melhor opção de compra possível, é diversão garantida a um preço justo e acessível, não tem erro)

[h1] Fun, challenging, deep content, engaging [/h1]

Points of interest as follows;
[list]
[*]Can move freely and choose your engagements.
[*]You can bail, finish or evade an area if you choose.
[*]Balance is done well.
[*]Many hero's to do runs with, and many to unlock
[*]Gold is hard to come by until you log enough losses and build up coin
[*]Maps can be explored freely, so far, no fog of war in 3 hours
[*]There is much depth to upgrades, play styles, and village rest area buildings of various uses.
[*]In later harder stages, the card reward or item reward during runs, the RNG becomes poor. Many of the repeat cards that do not help your build, and a waste of effort. Too frequent the RNG scales back and gives you crap. 
Needs adjustment.
[*]You can combine cards, to make them super mega powerful.
[*]Optimized and runs well on any pc.
[*]You can turn off blood and gore if you wish

[h1]Complaints[/h1]
[*]MUCH NEEDED a "hover over icons" to be reminded of benefits or what they do. Not the ones on the lower left, the TOP tray of icons on the left. 
In the top left of the screen during runs, nothing happens when you click or hover, and most of the time, we forget what they do. Needs to be fixed so you, as the player, are informed about what you have and why. Helps determine your build, but if, like me, you're new and/or forget, it sucks. I know what the lower ones do by hovering, so why not all of them? Silly really.
[*]Many items in the tavern or place where you chose your heroes, only a few are shown; you can show the others and unlock them, and again, hover over them to see what that hero does. does not exist. So far, you know nothing until you are unlocked.
[*][strike]It would be nice to know in advance, with a confirmation perhaps, the new cost of the card, before combining it. So many times, we were locked without enough mana to ever use it, because we had no idea what it would jump to.[/strike] It is there but not clear. Needs better.
[*]Having to go to the settings page to close the game is just stupid. We hit escape and had to go through so much to find out how to close the game. You must go to the settings in the village to close the game and exit.

[h1] All in all, this is a solid game, worth the price of admission, but the hover and detail change is needed badly to see what you have, not a hard implementation [/h1]

I have played a bunch of roguelike deckbuilders in the past and this might be the first time I genuinely think the game suffers from a lack of challenge. I am aware I haven't even unlocked the final areas, but I just beat Gallo Tower where I farmed up my curse to 1600% (which apparently gives enemies 16x stats) and my only concern was to make sure I get enough money to make this actually worth my while. Even the bosses in this game are complete fodder and the only notable boss (RedDeath) requires a bit of farming to take down. It infinitely respawns and gets a free hit, ensuring you can't literally fight him forever (you can actually but what's the point?)

I haven't even unlocked everything, I just got the jeweler unlocked and I'm just scratching my head at just how much power this dev hands to the player. I guess you can try to intentionally handicap yourself but unless you completely remove wild cards from the game I don't even know if this is ever hard. Heck, I'm not even convinced those would be necessary to be completely busted.

Idk, its kinda fun at the start but this is literally just ipad kid gameplay watch the screen explode. If you can have fun with that, by all means, but for me I just spent my last few runs just trying to skip everything so that its finally over.

Ich habe wirklich lange überlegt, ob ich einen Daumen nach oben gebe... Ich kann's leider nicht. 

Es ist ein Successor des grandiosen Vampire Survivors und es hat die Elemente aus dem Spiel wunderbar in dieses Spiel adaptiert. Es ist ein Vampire Survivors in einen Dungeon Crawler gepackt. Aber hier gibt es leider keinen Dungeon Crawler. 

Ich habe alle Hebel, Knöpfe, Falltüren, Tore, Fallen, Geheimnisse und Schalter in diesem Spiel zusammengezählt und es waren insgesamt: 

Drei sich selbst öffnende Tore vor dem Endboss Kampf. 

Wie TRAURIG, dabei hätte dieses Spiel SO VIEL POTENTIAL gehabt!!! Ein Hebel hinter einem Elitekampf, der das Tor zu einem Raum mit 3 Schatztruhen öffnet. Eine komplette Etage in der Bibliothek mit nur einer Wand, an der das Bild vom Knoblauch Helden hängt. Und wenn man da rauf drückt, dann öffnet sich ein Geheimgang zu besagtem Helden. Noch und NÖCHER! 

Nix. 

Kein bisschen Dungeon Crawler. 

Wie... Schade... 

Aber ja, wer Vampire Survivors 2 haben will, greift zu.

Somewhat entry-level deckbuilder that places fun over brutal difficulty. 

Like it's counterpart Vampire Survivors, it's about finding the path to becoming an ezclap yeet-machine and then enjoying the ride.

THE GOOD
[list]
    [*]No grinding.
    [*]Much better presentation than VS.
    [*]Great audio and animation / pacing.
    [*]Good controller support. (I would change your dpad left/right to turn, trust me)
    [*]A few solid music bangers.
    [*]Innovative UNO style combo system freshens the overdone block/attack system of other DB games.
    [*]There's a panda that sounds like he is saying "M#####F#####" (as pointed out by my friend)
[/list]

THE BAD
[list]
    [*]No grinding.😏
    [*]There are some arcanas/characters that trivialize the game after a bit. Yes you can turn it off but I wouldn't mind if they added some challenges or seeded runs that forced you back to just making the best of what you have or doing things you otherwise would not do.
    [*]Maybe fixed by now but confirm button on drafting a card will sometimes play a card too(double hit)
    [*]The final boss was one hit away from killing me but I still beat him first try. It could be dialed up a bit considering the arsenal you have, but maybe that's what DLC is for ;) 
     [*]I would have paid 3x the price for a 2x as long game. It's not short but feels a bit on the short side.
[/list]

Decided to review after I got 100% achievements so here I am.

Very simple: If you like games like Slay The Spire, Hearthstone or any other card battlers this is a good game for you. There's strategic play early on, once you get further it gets more crazy and ridiculous once you find some good combos.

Without a guide you're lowkey boned. Not sure why the steam achievements don't have the same UI tooltips/tracking as in the game itself or why some items/cards seem to have stupidly low droprates.

It took me legitimately 30 hours, just by pure chance or RNG, to find a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ stone mask which was needed for a literal chain of achieves. Aside from that, I have no gripes with the actual gameplay. It's solid, there's plenty to unlock and grind, keeps you busy for a good bit of hours for a 10€ game (I got 30+ hours out of it which is more than solid). 

Once you beat the real boss you can still try to kill the reaper for fun which isn't an achievement for some reason (to which I haven't bothered to go for it). If you're the kind of person to find random challenges to do etc this game will keep you busy for far longer. If you want to get achievements, you'll be in for at least 25-35 hours depending on RNG.

Not sure if any mod support will ever show up or if they expand the game but for now it's good as is. The only real negative reviews I've noticed is with UI issues which I had no real problems with. There's a fat pause icon that speeds you to the menu to leave the game, should be simple enough.

T:;dr decent card battler/dungeon crawling experience with lots of content for a relatively cheap price.

So, as a card battler, the "Final Solution" seems to come quick, and there isn't much change in your deck builds. You pretty much build towards your ideal deck, and repeat the same plays over again. It gets almost mindless. The satisfaction of "Solving the Puzzle" is missing along with a little bit of the creativity of exploring different builds.

-- Gameplay

Vampire Crawlers is a deckbuilding roguelike where every card has a mana cost, typically ranging from 0 to 5. Modifiers can drastically change these values, reducing costs into negatives or increasing them significantly. There are also special effects, such as removing mana costs entirely or enabling entire hands to be played for free under certain conditions.

Building your deck carefully is essential. Focusing too much on utility cards like mana generation, luck, or experience boosts can leave you without enough damage to survive, especially in higher-level stages.

Combat is flexible. You can eliminate enemies, push them back, freeze them, or rely on defensive strategies. However, defense builds require specific cards to be effective, particularly those that allow defensive values to persist between turns.

At first, the system may feel restrictive due to resets in mana, hand size, and combos between turns. However, once you understand the mechanics, it opens the door to extremely powerful builds. It is possible to reach massive combo chains, drawing dozens of cards in a single turn and stacking huge stat bonuses like attack, luck, or defense.

-- Progression and Systems

The game features a central hub where you can upgrade base stats such as attack, defense, experience gain, and luck. You can also choose different characters, each with unique weapons and abilities.

The blacksmith allows you to enhance cards by adding modifier slots and upgrading gem rarity. Higher-tier gems appear more frequently in later floors, encouraging long term progression and build optimization.

Each run takes place across multiple floors, usually around five per area, filled with enemies, destructible objects, and resources. Breaking objects is important for gathering extra items like healing or currency.

Key resources include additional mana and evolution items that significantly enhance your cards, allowing for powerful combinations.

-- Combat Depth

The game features two layers of stats: one that persists throughout the level and another that resets after each combat. Managing both effectively is crucial for success.

Card draw and mana reset between turns create a rhythm that may feel slow at first but is necessary to prevent the game from becoming unmanageable once high-level combos are achieved.

At extreme levels, managing large hands becomes a challenge. With 40 or more cards in hand, it can be difficult to quickly find the right card to continue a combo. A sorting system would greatly improve this aspect.

There is also a risk system where cards can break during extended combos, permanently removing them from your deck and spawning additional enemies. This adds tension to high-level play.

-- Exploration and Structure

The game uses a first-person perspective, which adds a unique feel to exploration. Movement and navigation are comfortable, and accessibility options help adjust the experience if needed.

Each level has a semi-randomized layout, keeping exploration fresh even within the same environment themes.

The minimap can be upgraded to reveal enemies, items, and resources, making navigation more efficient over time.

-- Audio and Visuals

The art style fits the game very well, and animations and effects are satisfying, especially during combat.

The soundtrack is a standout feature. Even after dozens of hours, it remains enjoyable and never feels repetitive.

Sound effects are also well done, giving weight to attacks and interactions with the environment.

-- Content and Replayability

The game offers a large amount of replayability through multiple characters, cards, builds, and modifiers. Runs can vary significantly in length, from around 30 minutes to several hours depending on playstyle.

There is also a useful feature that allows you to exit the game and resume your run later, which is especially helpful for longer sessions.

While much of the content is inspired by familiar elements, it still feels fresh thanks to the gameplay perspective and systems.

-- Difficulty

Difficulty is well balanced overall. As long as you understand the mechanics and invest in upgrades, progression feels fair. Farming and experimentation are key to mastering the game.

-- Final Thoughts

Vampire Crawlers is a highly engaging deckbuilding roguelike with deep systems and strong replayability. Its mechanics may feel unusual at first, but once understood, they allow for extremely satisfying and powerful builds.

With excellent music, solid visuals, and a gameplay loop that keeps you coming back for more, it is easy to lose track of time while playing. A few quality-of-life improvements would enhance the experience, but overall it is a very enjoyable and addictive game.

Extremely fun, but perhaps a bit too difficult for me. I am nevertheless giving it a thumbs up but would like to add my personal gripes in case it helps as feedback.

The move away from micromanagement play toward generalized deck building with a Play All function is very refreshing. I managed to finish all the way to the first level of the Tower at level 6 difficulty, but that's generally where I hit a brick wall.

Enemies started hitting far to high, and most of my attempts end on floor 1 before I could even get enough armours just to get by. I haven't maxed everything in the Upgrades and I haven't started upgrading things at the Blacksmith either, but doing that would mean going to grind lower levels for an obscene amount of time and it feels that perhaps that's where I'm dropping out.

I also don't really enjoy the "eye" system; I didn't quite grasp what it's called, but it seems to be a way to combat infinite builds or mana ramping, as the enemy's "turn" will take place after a certain number of cards are played, rather than when you end your turn yourself. I get the general idea, but frankly in a game like this it feels counterproductive. The purpose of a game like this is exactly to exploit the limits of the game and the rules, and break the system through creative means. It also grinds Play All to a screeching halt, making fights last significantly longer than they ever need to, even when it's clear the boss has absolutely zero chance of beating you either way.

Of course, I'm not asking for a "kill boss instantly if the math proves it can't beat me" button or anything like that, but creating an alternate system that makes fighting the boss *annoying* instead doesn't seem like a good use of my time either.

Lastly, and this is just a very mild, minor observation, but there seems to be random slowdowns / framerate dips in huge card combos sometimes?? I don't know why it happens. It doesn't bother me too much, but I'm running a 5080 and surely this game isn't tanking it, so it feels like there's some weirdness somewhere.
